questions.q: What is a B-tree index optimized for?
options: Exact match lookups only
Range queries, equality searches, and sorted traversal
Storing large text documents
Bitmap compression in data warehouses
questions.explanation: B-trees support range queries (BETWEEN), equality checks, and sorted iteration through self-balancing structure. Hash indexes handle exact matches but not ranges; B-trees are the standard choice for relational databases.

questions.q: What is the main difference between a clustered and non-clustered index?
options: Clustered indexes are faster; non-clustered indexes are slower
A clustered index determines the physical sort order of the table; a non-clustered index is a separate structure
Clustered indexes use B-trees; non-clustered indexes use hash tables
You can have unlimited clustered indexes but only 999 non-clustered indexes
questions.explanation: A clustered index reorders the table's physical storage; a table has only one clustered index. Non-clustered indexes are separate structures containing pointers to data rows. Speed depends on query type, not index type.

questions.q: A covering index is one that:
options: Includes all tables in a database
Contains all columns needed to satisfy a query without accessing the base table
Has more than one million rows of data
Uses multiple CPU cores to speed up queries
questions.explanation: A covering index stores all necessary columns so the query engine never needs to access the table itself, eliminating expensive lookups. This reduces I/O significantly but increases index storage.

questions.q: When is it beneficial to create an index on a column?
options: When the column is frequently used in WHERE clauses and has high cardinality
When the table has fewer than 100 rows
On every column in a table for maximum performance
Only on primary keys and foreign keys
questions.explanation: Indexes benefit queries filtering on high-cardinality columns with good selectivity. Small tables don't need indexes; indexing every column wastes space and slows writes. Selective indexing balances read and write performance.

options: An index stored on multiple database servers
An index on multiple columns, with key order mattering for query performance
An index created on a view instead of a table
An encrypted index that requires decryption before use
questions.explanation: A composite (multi-column) index indexes multiple columns together. Column order matters; (A, B) helps queries filtering on A or both A and B but not queries filtering only on B. Single-column queries benefit based on leftmost prefix.

questions.q: How do indexes impact INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations?
options: They have no impact on write performance
They improve write performance by caching data
They slow down writes because the index must also be updated
They only affect SELECT performance
questions.explanation: Each index must be updated when data changes, increasing write overhead. More indexes mean slower INSERTs, UPDATEs, and DELETEs. This tradeoff must be balanced against improved query performance.

options: The ability to index only selected tables
The percentage of rows that match an index condition, affecting query optimization
The number of indexes allowed per table
The process of choosing which columns to index
questions.explanation: Selectivity measures how many rows an index condition filters (0-100%). High selectivity (few matching rows) makes indexes very valuable; low selectivity wastes index resources. The query optimizer considers selectivity when choosing execution plans.

questions.q: What problem do indexes solve for query performance?
options: They reduce CPU usage by using less memory
They eliminate the need for the query optimizer
They provide fast row lookup and reduce the need for table scans
They automatically compress data to save disk space
questions.explanation: Indexes enable quick location of specific rows without scanning the entire table, dramatically improving query speed for filtered queries. They don't reduce memory, replace the optimizer, or compress data.

questions.q: In a database with low disk I/O capacity, why might you limit the number of indexes?
options: Indexes use no disk space
More indexes mean more data to read during writes, increasing I/O overhead
Indexes prevent parallel query execution
Database administrators have a hard limit on total indexes allowed
questions.explanation: Every index occupies disk space and must be updated on writes, consuming I/O bandwidth. With limited I/O capacity, excessive indexes create contention and slow both reads and writes. Strategic indexing maximizes I/O efficiency.

questions.q: What does a hash index excel at compared to B-tree indexes?
options: Range queries and BETWEEN conditions
Exact match lookups with O(1) average-case complexity
Sorted traversal and ORDER BY operations
Sparse data with many NULL values
questions.explanation: Hash indexes are optimized for equality checks (= operator) with constant-time lookups. B-trees handle ranges and sorting but are slower for exact matches. Hash indexes cannot support BETWEEN, LIKE, or ORDER BY.
